question:
5. Let \( f(x) \) be a real-valued function, defined for \( -1<x<1 \), such that \( f^{\prime}(0) \) exists. Let \( a_{n}, b_{n} \) be two sequences such that
\[
-1<a_{n}<0<b_{n}<1, \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} a_{n}=0, \lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} b_{n}=0
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f\left(b_{n}\right)-f\left(a_{n}\right)}{b_{n}-a_{n}}=f^{\prime}(0) . \quad \quad \quad \text { page 397) }
\]
solution:
Solution. Let \( \epsilon>0 \) be given. Then we can choose \( \delta, 0<\delta<1 \), so that for all \( x \) with \( 0<|x|<\delta \),
\[
\left|\frac{f(x)-f(0)}{x}-f^{\prime}(0)\right|<\epsilon .
\]

Let \( k \) be chosen so that, for all \( n \geq k \),
\[
\left|a_{n}\right|<\delta, \quad \text { and } \quad\left|b_{n}\right|<\delta .
\]

Then for \( m \geq k \) we have
\[
\left|\frac{f\left(a_{m}\right)-f(0)}{a_{m}}-f^{\prime}(0)\right|<\epsilon, \quad\left|\frac{f\left(b_{m}\right)-f(0)}{b_{m}}-f^{\prime}(0)\right|<\epsilon .
\]

But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mid f\left(b_{m}\right)-f\left(a_{m}\right) & -\left(b_{m}-a_{m}\right) f^{\prime}(0) \mid \\
& =\left|\left(f\left(b_{m}\right)-f(0)-b_{m} f^{\prime}(0)\right)-\left(f\left(a_{m}\right)-f(0)-a_{m} f^{\prime}(0)\right)\right| \\
& \leq\left|f\left(b_{m}\right)-f(0)-b_{m} f^{\prime}(0)\right|+\left|f\left(a_{m}\right)-f(0)-a_{m} f^{\prime}(0)\right| \\
& \leq \epsilon\left|b_{m}\right|+\epsilon\left|a_{m}\right|=\epsilon\left(b_{m}-a_{m}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

The penultimate step follows from (1) and the last from \( a_{m}<0<b_{m} \).
Therefore
\[
\left|\frac{f\left(b_{m}\right)-f\left(a_{m}\right)}{b_{m}-a_{m}}-f^{\prime}(0)\right|<\epsilon
\]
for all \( m>k \). Since this is true for any \( \epsilon>0 \), we have proved that
\[
\lim _{n \rightarrow \infty} \frac{f\left(b_{n}\right)-f\left(a_{n}\right)}{b_{n}-a_{n}}=f^{\prime}(0) .
\]

descriptive_long.question:
5. Let \( function(varitem) \) be a real-valued function, defined for \( -1<varitem<1 \), such that \( function^{\prime}(0) \) exists. Let \( seqleft_{indexer}, seqright_{indexer} \) be two sequences such that
\[
-1<seqleft_{indexer}<0<seqright_{indexer}<1, \lim _{indexer \rightarrow \infty} seqleft_{indexer}=0, \lim _{indexer \rightarrow \infty} seqright_{indexer}=0
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{indexer \rightarrow \infty} \frac{function\left(seqright_{indexer}\right)-function\left(seqleft_{indexer}\right)}{seqright_{indexer}-seqleft_{indexer}}=function^{\prime}(0) . \quad \quad \quad \text { page 397) }
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
Solution. Let \( accuracy>0 \) be given. Then we can choose \( smallrange, 0<smallrange<1 \), so that for all \( varitem \) with \( 0<|varitem|<smallrange \),
\[
\left|\frac{function(varitem)-function(0)}{varitem}-function^{\prime}(0)\right|<accuracy .
\]

Let \( threshold \) be chosen so that, for all \( indexer \geq threshold \),
\[
\left|seqleft_{indexer}\right|<smallrange, \quad \text { and } \quad\left|seqright_{indexer}\right|<smallrange .
\]

Then for \( subindex \geq threshold \) we have
\[
\left|\frac{function\left(seqleft_{subindex}\right)-function(0)}{seqleft_{subindex}}-function^{\prime}(0)\right|<accuracy, \quad\left|\frac{function\left(seqright_{subindex}\right)-function(0)}{seqright_{subindex}}-function^{\prime}(0)\right|<accuracy .
\]

But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mid function\left(seqright_{subindex}\right)-function\left(seqleft_{subindex}\right) & -\left(seqright_{subindex}-seqleft_{subindex}\right) function^{\prime}(0) \mid \\
& =\left|\left(function\left(seqright_{subindex}\right)-function(0)-seqright_{subindex} function^{\prime}(0)\right)-\left(function\left(seqleft_{subindex}\right)-function(0)-seqleft_{subindex} function^{\prime}(0)\right)\right| \\
& \leq\left|function\left(seqright_{subindex}\right)-function(0)-seqright_{subindex} function^{\prime}(0)\right|+\left|function\left(seqleft_{subindex}\right)-function(0)-seqleft_{subindex} function^{\prime}(0)\right| \\
& \leq accuracy\left|seqright_{subindex}\right|+accuracy\left|seqleft_{subindex}\right|=accuracy\left(seqright_{subindex}-seqleft_{subindex}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

The penultimate step follows from (1) and the last from \( seqleft_{subindex}<0<seqright_{subindex} \).
Therefore
\[
\left|\frac{function\left(seqright_{subindex}\right)-function\left(seqleft_{subindex}\right)}{seqright_{subindex}-seqleft_{subindex}}-function^{\prime}(0)\right|<accuracy
\]
for all \( subindex>threshold \). Since this is true for any \( accuracy>0 \), we have proved that
\[
\lim _{indexer \rightarrow \infty} \frac{function\left(seqright_{indexer}\right)-function\left(seqleft_{indexer}\right)}{seqright_{indexer}-seqleft_{indexer}}=function^{\prime}(0) .
\]

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
5. Let \( hedgehog(lampposts) \) be a real-valued function, defined for \( -1<lampposts<1 \), such that \( hedgehog^{\prime}(0) \) exists. Let \( breadcrumb_{raincloud}, marigolds_{raincloud} \) be two sequences such that
\[
-1<breadcrumb_{raincloud}<0<marigolds_{raincloud}<1, \lim _{raincloud \rightarrow \infty} breadcrumb_{raincloud}=0, \lim _{raincloud \rightarrow \infty} marigolds_{raincloud}=0
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{raincloud \rightarrow \infty} \frac{hedgehog\left(marigolds_{raincloud}\right)-hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{raincloud}\right)}{marigolds_{raincloud}-breadcrumb_{raincloud}}=hedgehog^{\prime}(0) . \quad \quad \quad \text { page 397) }
\]
descriptive_long_confusing.solution:
Solution. Let \( blueberry>0 \) be given. Then we can choose \( rainwater, 0<rainwater<1 \), so that for all \( lampposts \) with \( 0<|lampposts|<rainwater \),
\[
\left|\frac{hedgehog(lampposts)-hedgehog(0)}{lampposts}-hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right|<blueberry .
\]

Let \( dandelion \) be chosen so that, for all \( raincloud \geq dandelion \),
\[
\left|breadcrumb_{raincloud}\right|<rainwater, \quad \text { and } \quad\left|marigolds_{raincloud}\right|<rainwater .
\]

Then for \( sunflower \geq dandelion \) we have
\[
\left|\frac{hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)}{breadcrumb_{sunflower}}-hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right|<blueberry, \quad\left|\frac{hedgehog\left(marigolds_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)}{marigolds_{sunflower}}-hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right|<blueberry .
\]

But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mid hedgehog\left(marigolds_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right) & -\left(marigolds_{sunflower}-breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right) hedgehog^{\prime}(0) \mid \\
& =\left|\left(hedgehog\left(marigolds_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)-marigolds_{sunflower} hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right)-\left(hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)-breadcrumb_{sunflower} hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right)\right| \\
& \leq\left|hedgehog\left(marigolds_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)-marigolds_{sunflower} hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right|+\left|hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog(0)-breadcrumb_{sunflower} hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right| \\
& \leq blueberry\left|marigolds_{sunflower}\right|+blueberry\left|breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right|=blueberry\left(marigolds_{sunflower}-breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

The penultimate step follows from (1) and the last from \( breadcrumb_{sunflower}<0<marigolds_{sunflower} \).
Therefore
\[
\left|\frac{hedgehog\left(marigolds_{sunflower}\right)-hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{sunflower}\right)}{marigolds_{sunflower}-breadcrumb_{sunflower}}-hedgehog^{\prime}(0)\right|<blueberry
\]
for all \( sunflower>dandelion \). Since this is true for any \( blueberry>0 \), we have proved that
\[
\lim _{raincloud \rightarrow \infty} \frac{hedgehog\left(marigolds_{raincloud}\right)-hedgehog\left(breadcrumb_{raincloud}\right)}{marigolds_{raincloud}-breadcrumb_{raincloud}}=hedgehog^{\prime}(0) .
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
5. Let \( malfunction(constantval) \) be a real-valued function, defined for \( -1<constantval<1 \), such that \( malfunction^{\prime}(0) \) exists. Let \( positivepeak, negativepit \) be two sequences such that
\[
-1<positivepeak<0<negativepit<1, \lim _{fixedcount \rightarrow \infty} positivepeak=0, \lim _{fixedcount \rightarrow \infty} negativepit=0
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{fixedcount \rightarrow \infty} \frac{malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)}{negativepit-positivepeak}=malfunction^{\prime}(0) . \quad \quad \quad \text { page 397) }
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
Solution. Let \( vastamount>0 \) be given. Then we can choose \( largespread, 0<largespread<1 \), so that for all \( constantval \) with \( 0<|constantval|<largespread \),
\[
\left|\frac{malfunction(constantval)-malfunction(0)}{constantval}-malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right|<vastamount .
\]

Let \( motionless \) be chosen so that, for all \( fixedcount \geq motionless \),
\[
\left|positivepeak\right|<largespread, \quad \text { and } \quad\left|negativepit\right|<largespread .
\]

Then for \( rigidmark \geq motionless \) we have
\[
\left|\frac{malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)-malfunction(0)}{positivepeak}-malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right|<vastamount, \quad\left|\frac{malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction(0)}{negativepit}-malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right|<vastamount .
\]

But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mid malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction\left(positivepeak\right) & -\left(negativepit-positivepeak\right) malfunction^{\prime}(0) \mid \\
& =\left|\left(malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction(0)-negativepit malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right)-\left(malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)-malfunction(0)-positivepeak malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right)\right| \\
& \leq\left|malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction(0)-negativepit malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right|+\left|malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)-malfunction(0)-positivepeak malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right| \\
& \leq vastamount\left|negativepit\right|+vastamount\left|positivepeak\right|=vastamount\left(negativepit-positivepeak\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

The penultimate step follows from (1) and the last from \( positivepeak<0<negativepit \).
Therefore
\[
\left|\frac{malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)}{negativepit-positivepeak}-malfunction^{\prime}(0)\right|<vastamount
\]
for all \( rigidmark>motionless \). Since this is true for any \( vastamount>0 \), we have proved that
\[
\lim _{fixedcount \rightarrow \infty} \frac{malfunction\left(negativepit\right)-malfunction\left(positivepeak\right)}{negativepit-positivepeak}=malfunction^{\prime}(0) .
\]

garbled_string.question:
5. Let \( lbntxeyo(qzxwvtnp) \) be a real-valued function, defined for \( -1<qzxwvtnp<1 \), such that \( lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0) \) exists. Let \( fgdmrkyu, wqcslzha \) be two sequences such that
\[
-1<fgdmrkyu<0<wqcslzha<1, \lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} fgdmrkyu=0, \lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} wqcslzha=0
\]

Prove that
\[
\lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} \frac{lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)}{wqcslzha-fgdmrkyu}=lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0) . \quad \quad \quad \text { page 397) }
\]
garbled_string.solution:
Solution. Let \( zpjrwhad>0 \) be given. Then we can choose \( gvtmskui, 0<gvtmskui<1 \), so that for all \( qzxwvtnp \) with \( 0<|qzxwvtnp|<gvtmskui \),
\[
\left|\frac{lbntxeyo(qzxwvtnp)-lbntxeyo(0)}{qzxwvtnp}-lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right|<zpjrwhad .
\]

Let \( tmbsjepr \) be chosen so that, for all \( hjgrksla \geq tmbsjepr \),
\[
\left|fgdmrkyu\right|<gvtmskui, \quad \text { and } \quad\left|wqcslzha\right|<gvtmskui .
\]

Then for \( vdlqczno \geq tmbsjepr \) we have
\[
\left|\frac{lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)-lbntxeyo(0)}{fgdmrkyu}-lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right|<zpjrwhad, \quad\left|\frac{lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo(0)}{wqcslzha}-lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right|<zpjrwhad .
\]

But
\[
\begin{aligned}
\mid lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right) & -\left(wqcslzha-fgdmrkyu\right) lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0) \mid \\
& =\left|\left(lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo(0)-wqcslzha\, lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right)-\left(lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)-lbntxeyo(0)-fgdmrkyu\, lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right)\right| \\
& \leq\left|lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo(0)-wqcslzha\, lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right|+\left|lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)-lbntxeyo(0)-fgdmrkyu\, lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right| \\
& \leq zpjrwhad\left|wqcslzha\right|+zpjrwhad\left|fgdmrkyu\right|=zpjrwhad\left(wqcslzha-fgdmrkyu\right) .
\end{aligned}
\]

The penultimate step follows from (1) and the last from \( fgdmrkyu<0<wqcslzha \).
Therefore
\[
\left|\frac{lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)}{wqcslzha-fgdmrkyu}-lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0)\right|<zpjrwhad
\]
for all \( vdlqczno>tmbsjepr \). Since this is true for any \( zpjrwhad>0 \), we have proved that
\[
\lim _{hjgrksla \rightarrow \infty} \frac{lbntxeyo\left(wqcslzha\right)-lbntxeyo\left(fgdmrkyu\right)}{wqcslzha-fgdmrkyu}=lbntxeyo^{\prime}(0) .
\]

kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Basic notation  
Put  

 h_n := b_n - a_n (so that r_n = \|h_n\| and u_n = h_n / r_n),   
 m_n := (a_n + b_n)/2.

Hence a_n = m_n - \frac{1}{2} h_n and b_n = m_n + \frac{1}{2} h_n.

Step 1.  Second-order expansion about the single point 0  
Because of (\star ) we have, for every vector x near 0,  

 f(x) = f(0) + A(x) + \frac{1}{2} B(x,x) + o(\|x\|^2). (2)

Apply (2) to x = a_n, m_n, b_n:

 f(a_n) = f(0) + A(a_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(a_n,a_n) + o(\|a_n\|^2),  
 f(m_n) = f(0) + A(m_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(m_n,m_n) + o(\|m_n\|^2), (3)  
 f(b_n) = f(0) + A(b_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(b_n,b_n) + o(\|b_n\|^2).

The three little-o terms are with respect to the single limit h\searrow 0; they depend only on the arguments a_n, m_n, b_n and hence are legitimate.

Step 2.  Form the discrete second difference  
Set  

 \Delta _n := f(b_n) - 2 f(m_n) + f(a_n).

Insert (3):

 \Delta _n = [A(b_n) - 2A(m_n) + A(a_n)]  
    + \frac{1}{2} [ B(b_n,b_n) - 2B(m_n,m_n) + B(a_n,a_n) ]  
    + o(\|a_n\|^2) + o(\|m_n\|^2) + o(\|b_n\|^2).   (4)

(a)  Cancellation of the linear terms.  
Because A is linear and m_n = (a_n + b_n)/2,

 A(b_n) - 2A(m_n) + A(a_n)  
 = A(b_n) + A(a_n) - A(a_n + b_n) = 0.   (5)

(b)  Evaluation of the quadratic bracket.  
Write b_n = m_n + \frac{1}{2} h_n and a_n = m_n - \frac{1}{2} h_n.  Using bilinearity and symmetry of B,

 B(b_n,b_n) = B(m_n,m_n) + B(m_n,h_n) + \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n),  
 B(a_n,a_n) = B(m_n,m_n) - B(m_n,h_n) + \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n).

Adding and subtracting 2 B(m_n,m_n) we get

 B(b_n,b_n) - 2B(m_n,m_n) + B(a_n,a_n) = \frac{1}{2} B(h_n,h_n).   (6)

Combining (4), (5) and (6),

 \Delta _n = \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n) + \rho _n,                          (7)

where \rho _n := o(\|a_n\|^2) + o(\|m_n\|^2) + o(\|b_n\|^2).

Step 3.  Size of the error term \rho _n  
Because \|a_n\| \leq  \|m_n\| + \frac{1}{2}\|h_n\| = o(r_n) + O(r_n) = O(r_n) and likewise \|b_n\| = O(r_n), each of \|a_n\|^2, \|b_n\|^2, \|m_n\|^2 is O(r_n^2).  Consequently

 \rho _n = o(r_n^2).                                  (8)

Step 4.  Normalisation  

 4 \Delta _n / r_n^2 = 4\cdot [\frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n)] / r_n^2 + 4\rho _n / r_n^2  
              = B(h_n/\|h_n\|, h_n/\|h_n\|) + o(1)  
              = B(u_n,u_n) + o(1).                (9)

Step 5.  Passage to the limit  
Because u_n \to  u by assumption, continuity of the quadratic form yields

 lim_{n\to \infty } B(u_n,u_n) = B(u,u).                  (10)

From (9) and (10) we finally obtain

 lim_{n\to \infty } 4 \cdot  [ f(b_n) - 2 f(m_n) + f(a_n) ] / r_n^2  
         = B(u,u),                                 as required. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher order: The original and kernel variants concern first derivatives; the enhanced problem requires understanding and manipulating second Fréchet derivatives (Hessians).

2. Higher dimensions: The setting is ℝᵏ (arbitrary dimension) instead of the real line.

3. Additional sequences & geometry: Two sequences produce a shrinking chord whose midpoint also approaches the base point subject to a subtle “midpoint =o(chord)” condition, forcing control of non-collinearity and necessitating a careful geometric decomposition a_n = m_n − ½ h_n, b_n = m_n + ½ h_n.

4. Sophisticated tools: The solution demands
   • Multivariable Taylor expansions with Peano-type remainders,  
   • Uniform control of remainders under simultaneous limits,  
   • Operator–norm continuity of derivatives, and  
   • Coordination of several converging objects (h_n, m_n, u_n).

5. More steps & deeper insight: One must (i) reformulate the chord in terms of midpoint and direction, (ii) expand f at a moving base point m_n (not fixed at 0), (iii) show cancellation of first-order terms, (iv) normalise correctly, and (v) pass limits through bilinear forms—each a non-trivial step absent from the original exercise.

Altogether these features make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original and the current kernel variant.

original_kernel_variant.solution:
Step 0.  Basic notation  
Put  

 h_n := b_n - a_n (so that r_n = \|h_n\| and u_n = h_n / r_n),   
 m_n := (a_n + b_n)/2.

Hence a_n = m_n - \frac{1}{2} h_n and b_n = m_n + \frac{1}{2} h_n.

Step 1.  Second-order expansion about the single point 0  
Because of (\star ) we have, for every vector x near 0,  

 f(x) = f(0) + A(x) + \frac{1}{2} B(x,x) + o(\|x\|^2). (2)

Apply (2) to x = a_n, m_n, b_n:

 f(a_n) = f(0) + A(a_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(a_n,a_n) + o(\|a_n\|^2),  
 f(m_n) = f(0) + A(m_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(m_n,m_n) + o(\|m_n\|^2), (3)  
 f(b_n) = f(0) + A(b_n) + \frac{1}{2} B(b_n,b_n) + o(\|b_n\|^2).

The three little-o terms are with respect to the single limit h\searrow 0; they depend only on the arguments a_n, m_n, b_n and hence are legitimate.

Step 2.  Form the discrete second difference  
Set  

 \Delta _n := f(b_n) - 2 f(m_n) + f(a_n).

Insert (3):

 \Delta _n = [A(b_n) - 2A(m_n) + A(a_n)]  
    + \frac{1}{2} [ B(b_n,b_n) - 2B(m_n,m_n) + B(a_n,a_n) ]  
    + o(\|a_n\|^2) + o(\|m_n\|^2) + o(\|b_n\|^2).   (4)

(a)  Cancellation of the linear terms.  
Because A is linear and m_n = (a_n + b_n)/2,

 A(b_n) - 2A(m_n) + A(a_n)  
 = A(b_n) + A(a_n) - A(a_n + b_n) = 0.   (5)

(b)  Evaluation of the quadratic bracket.  
Write b_n = m_n + \frac{1}{2} h_n and a_n = m_n - \frac{1}{2} h_n.  Using bilinearity and symmetry of B,

 B(b_n,b_n) = B(m_n,m_n) + B(m_n,h_n) + \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n),  
 B(a_n,a_n) = B(m_n,m_n) - B(m_n,h_n) + \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n).

Adding and subtracting 2 B(m_n,m_n) we get

 B(b_n,b_n) - 2B(m_n,m_n) + B(a_n,a_n) = \frac{1}{2} B(h_n,h_n).   (6)

Combining (4), (5) and (6),

 \Delta _n = \frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n) + \rho _n,                          (7)

where \rho _n := o(\|a_n\|^2) + o(\|m_n\|^2) + o(\|b_n\|^2).

Step 3.  Size of the error term \rho _n  
Because \|a_n\| \leq  \|m_n\| + \frac{1}{2}\|h_n\| = o(r_n) + O(r_n) = O(r_n) and likewise \|b_n\| = O(r_n), each of \|a_n\|^2, \|b_n\|^2, \|m_n\|^2 is O(r_n^2).  Consequently

 \rho _n = o(r_n^2).                                  (8)

Step 4.  Normalisation  

 4 \Delta _n / r_n^2 = 4\cdot [\frac{1}{4} B(h_n,h_n)] / r_n^2 + 4\rho _n / r_n^2  
              = B(h_n/\|h_n\|, h_n/\|h_n\|) + o(1)  
              = B(u_n,u_n) + o(1).                (9)

Step 5.  Passage to the limit  
Because u_n \to  u by assumption, continuity of the quadratic form yields

 lim_{n\to \infty } B(u_n,u_n) = B(u,u).                  (10)

From (9) and (10) we finally obtain

 lim_{n\to \infty } 4 \cdot  [ f(b_n) - 2 f(m_n) + f(a_n) ] / r_n^2  
         = B(u,u),                                 as required. \blacksquare 



------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------

metadata.difficulty_analysis:
1. Higher order: The original and kernel variants concern first derivatives; the enhanced problem requires understanding and manipulating second Fréchet derivatives (Hessians).

2. Higher dimensions: The setting is ℝᵏ (arbitrary dimension) instead of the real line.

3. Additional sequences & geometry: Two sequences produce a shrinking chord whose midpoint also approaches the base point subject to a subtle “midpoint =o(chord)” condition, forcing control of non-collinearity and necessitating a careful geometric decomposition a_n = m_n − ½ h_n, b_n = m_n + ½ h_n.

4. Sophisticated tools: The solution demands
   • Multivariable Taylor expansions with Peano-type remainders,  
   • Uniform control of remainders under simultaneous limits,  
   • Operator–norm continuity of derivatives, and  
   • Coordination of several converging objects (h_n, m_n, u_n).

5. More steps & deeper insight: One must (i) reformulate the chord in terms of midpoint and direction, (ii) expand f at a moving base point m_n (not fixed at 0), (iii) show cancellation of first-order terms, (iv) normalise correctly, and (v) pass limits through bilinear forms—each a non-trivial step absent from the original exercise.

Altogether these features make the enhanced variant substantially harder than both the original and the current kernel variant.
